Farn Por-rrrcel PRACrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
May 22,2006
Chad A. Jacobs
Deputy City Attorney
City and County of San Francisco
City Hall - 1 Dr. Carlton B. Goodlett Place, Ste.234
San Francisco, CA 94102
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-06-076
Dear Mr. Jacobs:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of the San Francisco
Department of the Environment ("SF Environment') regarding the mass mailing
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act,,).1
QUESTTON
Does section 89001 prohibit SF Environment from using city funds to distribute a
recorded telephone message featuring an elected official affiliated with the department?
CONCLUSION
No. Section 89001 does not prohibit the distribution of recorded telephone
messages.
FACTS
SF Environment is responsible for conducting public education and outreach on
environmental issues including recycling. SF Environment has proposed using city funds
to distribute a recorded telephone message featuring an elected official affiliated with the
department urglng city residents to recycle. The telephone message would be distributed
by telephone to more than200 city residents in a calendar month.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appeff at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code ofRegulations.
LeE


File No. A-06-076
Page No. 2
The Act provides that no newsletter or mass mailing shall be sent at public
expense. (Section 89001.) A mass mailing is defined as "over two hundred substantially
similar pieces of mail, but does not include a form letter or other mail which is sent in
response to an unsolicited request, letter or other inquiry.,, (Secti on g2041.5.)
Regulation 18901 further defines the prohibition and provides discrete exceptions
from the mass mailirgblr.. Under regulation 18901, the prohibition applies if more than
200 tangsble items, which feature an elected officer affiliated with the ui*"y which
produces or sends the items, are sent in any calendar month, and the 
"osf of dlrt ibution,
or more than $50 of the costs of design, printing, or production, are paid with public
funds, unless an express exception applies.
Under current Commission advice, consistent with regulation 1g901, the ban is
applicable to tangible items only. By way of example, in prwious letters, the
Commission has advised that the ban is applicable io facsimile transmi.riorm because the
transmission results ]n the receipt of a paper copy. (Harmony Adviceletters, No. I-91-
254 and No. 4-94-135.) However, website postings and electronic mail -"r.ug"r, which
are not tangible, are not regulated by regulation 18901. (Cook Advice Letter, No. I-02-
723; Peterson Advice Letter, No. 4-99-013 Foote Advice Letter, No. A-gs-i 14; Doyle
Advice Letter, No. I-94-364.) Because a recorded telephone message does not result in
the receipt of a tangible item, the message would not be prohibited 6'y section g9001.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
ANALYSIS
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
(^I-- L<)n^"tU
By: CarlaWardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does section 89001 prohibit SF Environment from using city funds to distribute a
recorded telephone message featuring an elected official affiliated with the department?

CONCLUSION: No. Section 89001 does not prohibit the distribution of recorded telephone
messages.
